perturbation.original_text: The term "Evaluation Material" also shall be deemed to include all notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents prepared by each party or its Representatives which contain, reflect or are based upon, in whole or in part, the information furnished to such party or its Representatives pursuant hereto which is not available to the general public.
perturbation.changed_text: The term "Evaluation Material" shall include only the exact copies of documents furnished, excluding any notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other derivative documents prepared by either party or its Representatives. This definition applies regardless of whether the underlying information is available to the general public.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Evaluation Material', excluding derivative works and analyses, even if based on the original information. This creates a contradiction within the contract itself, as the original text implies a broader scope.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets Law - Definition of Trade Secret
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the broad interpretation of trade secrets law, which includes not only the original documents but also any analyses, compilations, or derivative works that are based on the confidential information and provide a competitive advantage. 18 U.S. Code § 1839 defines 'trade secret' in a way that encompasses derived information, while the modified contract specifically excludes it from the definition of 'Evaluation Material,' weakening trade secret protection.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text narrows the definition of 'Evaluation Material' to exclude derivative works, contradicting the broad interpretation of trade secrets law. The Law Explanation states that trade secrets law includes analyses, compilations, or derivative works based on confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2, citing 18 USC 1839, provides definitions related to trade secrets, supporting the claim that the modified contract's exclusion weakens trade secret protection by not encompassing derived information, leading to a clear non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereby agrees that it and its Representatives shall use the other party's Evaluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating, negotiating and consummating a possible transaction between the parties, and that the disclosing party's Evaluation Material will be kept confidential and each party and its Representatives will not disclose or use for purposes other than the evaluation, negotiation and consummation of a transaction any of the other party's Evaluation Material in any manner whatsoever
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to use the Evaluation Material for any legitimate business purpose, and there is no implied or explicit restriction on usage or disclosure. Confidentiality is encouraged, but not required, and parties shall not be liable for any unintentional use or disclosure of Evaluation Material.
perturbation.explanation: This modification significantly weakens the confidentiality obligations regarding the Evaluation Material, contradicting the original intention of the agreement to protect sensitive information during negotiations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification directly contradicts the UTSA, which protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By removing the confidentiality requirement and allowing use for 'any legitimate business purpose,' the modified text effectively eliminates trade secret protection, as misappropriation is typically defined as using or disclosing a trade secret without consent and in a manner that harms the owner's competitive advantage. This change makes the contract unenforceable in protecting confidential information under trade secret law. The UTSA defines 'Misappropriation' in section 1(4) which this change goes against.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens confidentiality obligations, contradicting the original intent to protect sensitive information. The Law Explanation states that this change 'effectively eliminates trade secret protection' and goes against UTSA 1(4) which defines 'Misappropriation'. The changed text allows use for 'any legitimate business purpose' and states that 'there is no implied or explicit restriction on usage or disclosure', and removes liability for 'unintentional use or disclosure of Evaluation Material'. This directly contradicts the original text which states Evaluation Material 'will be kept confidential' and will not be disclosed or used 'in any manner whatsoever'.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that a party or any of its Representatives are requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the other party's Evaluation Material, the party requested or required to make the disclosure shall provide the other party with prompt written notice of any such request or requirement so that the other party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: If a party or its Representatives are compelled to disclose Evaluation Material due to legal processes, they are not required to notify the other party. The disclosing party assumes full responsibility for compliance and any associated liabilities without recourse to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: This modification eliminates the requirement for notification in case of required disclosure, removing a key safeguard for the disclosing party to protect its confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While FRCP 45 primarily concerns subpoenas, the spirit of the rule and related case law emphasizes the importance of providing notice to parties whose information is being sought through legal process. Eliminating the notification requirement undermines the ability of the disclosing party to protect its interests and potentially seek a protective order, conflicting with principles of due process and fairness in legal proceedings. FRCP 45(a)(1)(D) requires a subpoena to 'command each person to whom it is directed to attend and testify,' implying notification so they can respond appropriately.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principles outlined in FRCP 45, which emphasizes providing notice to parties whose information is being sought through legal process. Specifically, the modification eliminates the requirement for notification in case of required disclosure, removing a key safeguard. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on the form and content of a subpoena, it states that a subpoena must "command each person to whom it is directed to attend and testify," implying notification. By removing the notification requirement, the modified text undermines the ability of the other party to protect its interests, seek a protective order, or respond appropriately, creating a legal contradiction. This falls under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' perturbation type as the contractual language deviates from the legally recognized need for notification in legal processes, making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of each party and its respective directors, officers, stockholders, affiliates, and agents, and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is solely for the benefit of the direct parties, Ricoh Company, Ltd. and Lanier Worldwide, Inc., and explicitly excludes any rights or benefits for directors, officers, stockholders, affiliates, or agents. This agreement shall be interpreted under the laws of the Principality of Sealand.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes third-party beneficiary rights and changes the governing law to a jurisdiction with questionable legal standing, creating uncertainty and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties are generally free to choose the governing law in a contract, New York courts may refuse to enforce a choice of law provision if the chosen jurisdiction has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, or if applying the chosen law would violate a fundamental principle of New York law. Selecting the 'laws of the Principality of Sealand' would almost certainly be deemed unreasonable and against public policy, leading a New York court to disregard the choice of law provision and apply New York law instead. Additionally, removing the benefit to directors etc goes against standard corporate practice.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions up TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions next SECTION 5-1402 Choice of forum This entry was published on 2018-08-31 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original contract's intent to extend benefits to parties beyond Ricoh Company, Ltd. and Lanier Worldwide, Inc., and also alters the governing law to the Principality of Sealand. This could be deemed unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY Gen. Oblig. Law § 5-1401, states that parties to a contract may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties. However, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, both raise concerns that the chosen jurisdiction ('laws of the Principality of Sealand') might be considered unreasonable, potentially leading a New York court to disregard the choice of law provision. Given this potential unenforceability and the removal of benefits to directors, officers, etc., the modification introduces a significant legal contradiction.
